title: Chrome descaler spray
subtitle: A citric acid spray for removing limescale from chrome taps, shower heads, and bathroom fittings
excerpt: Citric acid dissolved in water forms an effective descaler for chrome taps, shower heads, and bathroom fittings. Limescale is alkaline calcium carbonate; citric acid dissolves it on contact. Spray on, leave for 10 to 15 minutes, scrub lightly, and rinse. Effective on chrome, stainless steel, and glazed ceramic. Not suitable for marble, limestone, or other natural stone.
sourceNotes: Citric acid as a descaler: INCI, food-safe E330, widely used in commercial descaling products. Citric acid dissolves calcium carbonate (limescale) by forming soluble calcium citrate: standard chemistry reference. Safe for chrome and stainless steel at household concentrations (5 to 10% solution): standard household cleaning reference. Caution: do not use on marble, limestone, travertine, or unsealed grout; acid etches natural stone.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. A 250 ml bottle treats an entire bathroom in a single session.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stable for several months in a sealed spray bottle. Label with contents and date.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Keeps 6 months in a sealed spray bottle. Discard if the solution becomes cloudy or develops an unusual smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hard, chalky deposit of calcium carbonate left by hard water as it evaporates. Limescale is alkaline and dissolves readily in mild acids such as citric acid or white vinegar.

content.text: Citric acid is the active ingredient in most commercial bathroom descalers. At a 5 to 8% concentration in water it dissolves

quickly and leaves chrome and stainless steel fittings clean without scratching. It is food-safe at this concentration and leaves no toxic residue. Do not use this spray on marble, limestone, travertine, or other natural stone surfaces; the acid etches them.

attrs.title: Natural stone surfaces: do not use
attrs.body: Citric acid, like all acids, etches marble, limestone, travertine, and slate. Use only on chrome, stainless steel, glazed ceramic, and glass. If in doubt about a surface, test on a hidden area first.

items.prepNote: or cooled boiled water; reduces mineral deposits in the bottle
food-grade; available from home-brewing suppliers and supermarkets

content.text: Dissolve the citric acid powder in the water. Stir until completely dissolved; it dissolves quickly at room temperature. Pour into a spray bottle and label with contents and date.

Spray directly onto the limescale deposit on taps, shower heads, shower doors, or tiles. Leave for 10 to 15 minutes for light build-up; leave up to 30 minutes for thick deposits. Scrub lightly with a cloth or soft brush, then rinse thoroughly with clean water. For shower heads, fill a plastic bag with the solution, secure it around the head with an elastic band, and leave for 30 minutes before removing and rinsing.
Keep in a sealed spray bottle. Keeps 6 months. Discard if the solution becomes cloudy or smells unusual, which may indicate contamination.
